lessons.title: What Is a Stream?
lessons.summary: Learn the fundamental concepts of the Stream API. A Stream is not a collection but a flow of data that operates with lazy evaluation. Understand the pipeline structure of source → intermediate operations → terminal operations, and how to create Streams.

contents.title: What Is the Stream API?
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- What a Stream is (a data processing **pipeline**)
- How to create Streams (collection.stream(), Stream.of(), etc.)
- The rule that a Stream can only be used once

Learn the new paradigm of declarative data processing.

contents.title: Structure of a Stream Pipeline
contents.body:
Stream processing consists of three stages: "Source (creation) → **Intermediate Operations** → **Terminal Operation**".
The source is the data that feeds the Stream, generated from collections, [[arrays]], files, etc.
[[Intermediate operations]] transform the Stream, such as filter() and map(), and return a new Stream.
[[Terminal operations]] execute the Stream processing and return a result, such as forEach() and collect().
Intermediate operations are **lazily evaluated**, meaning the actual processing is not executed until a terminal operation is called.
A Stream cannot be reused once consumed, so calling a terminal operation twice on the same Stream throws an IllegalStateException.
contents.note: A Stream **pipeline** is a declarative style that describes "what to process." Traditional [[for loops]] use an imperative style that describes "how to process."

contents.title: Ways to Create a Stream
contents.body:
There are multiple ways to create a Stream.
| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| Collection.stream() | Creates a Stream from a collection (most common) |
| Stream.of() | Creates a Stream from specified elements |
| Arrays.stream() | Creates a Stream from an array |
| Stream.empty() | Creates an empty Stream |
You can also create infinite Streams with Stream.generate() or Stream.iterate().
Files.lines() can be used to obtain each line of a file as a Stream.
contents.note: The "//" in the code are comments (explanatory notes). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: Stream Characteristics and Caveats
contents.body:
Streams do not store data. They only process data through the **pipeline**.
A Stream can only be used once. An [[exception]] is thrown if you try to perform operations after a **terminal operation** has been executed.
**Intermediate operations** are **lazily evaluated**. They are executed only when a terminal operation is invoked.
Using parallelStream() enables parallel processing, but be careful with operations that are not [[thread]]-safe.
Streams do not modify the source data (non-destructive).
contents.note: If you want to use a Stream multiple times, you need to create a new Stream each time.

contents.title: Exam Prep: Stream Basics
contents.body:
The Gold exam tests whether you can distinguish between Stream creation, **intermediate operations**, and **terminal operations**.
Pay attention to the behavior when passing null to Stream.of(), and the difference between Stream.of(array) and Arrays.stream(array).
Stream.of(array) treats the entire array as a single element, while Arrays.stream(array) treats each array element as a Stream element.
Stream.iterate() also has an [[overload]] that takes a [[Predicate]] as a third argument (since Java 9).
In problems about **lazy evaluation**, the processing order of Streams using peek() is a frequent topic.

quizzes.question: Which of the following statements about Streams is correct?
quizzes.explanation: A Stream cannot be reused once a terminal operation has been executed. Attempting to reuse it throws an IllegalStateException. A Stream is not a collection that stores data, and intermediate operations are lazily evaluated, so they are not executed without a terminal operation.

choices.text: A Stream cannot be reused once a terminal operation has been executed
A Stream is a collection that stores data internally
Intermediate operations are executed even without a terminal operation

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: Since int[] is a primitive array, Stream.of(int[]) stores the int array itself as a single element in Stream<int[]>, and count() returns 1. On the other hand, Arrays.stream(int[]) has a dedicated overload that expands each element into an IntStream, so it returns 3. Note that for reference-type arrays like String[], Stream.of() also expands via varargs, so both would return 3.

quizzes.question: To create a Stream from a collection, call the collection's ___() method.
quizzes.code:
List<String> list = List.of("A", "B");
Stream<String> s = list.___();
quizzes.explanation: By calling the Collection.stream() method, you can create a sequential Stream containing the collection's elements. To create a parallel Stream, use parallelStream().

lessons.title: Intermediate Operations
lessons.summary: Learn about Stream intermediate operations: filter(), map(), flatMap(), distinct(), sorted(), and peek(). Understand that intermediate operations are lazily evaluated and not executed until a terminal operation is called.

contents.title: Overview of Intermediate Operations
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Narrowing elements with filter()
- Transforming elements with map()
- Using sorted(), distinct(), limit(), and skip()

**Intermediate operations** are **lazily evaluated** and are not executed until a **terminal operation** is called.

contents.title: filter() and map()
contents.body:
filter([[Predicate]]<T>) is an **intermediate operation** that passes only elements matching a condition.
Only elements for which the Predicate's test() returns true remain.
map([[Function]]<T,R>) is an [[intermediate operation]] that transforms each element into a different value.
The Function's apply() performs the transformation, and a Stream of the transformed type is returned.
filter() and map() are the most frequently used intermediate operations in Stream processing.
The order in which they are combined affects processing efficiency, so it is common to apply filter first and map afterward.
contents.note: It is more efficient to apply filter() first to narrow down the data and then transform with map().

contents.title: flatMap()
contents.body:
flatMap(Function<T, Stream<R>>) transforms each element into a Stream and flattens them into a single Stream.
It is very useful when dealing with nested structures (such as a list of lists).
While map() performs a one-to-one transformation, flatMap() performs a one-to-many transformation.
The function passed to flatMap() must return a Stream.
It is also possible to filter elements by returning an empty Stream.
contents.note: The "//" in the code are comments (explanatory notes). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: distinct(), sorted(), peek()
contents.body:
| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| distinct() | An intermediate operation that removes duplicate elements. Equality is determined by the equals() method. |
| sorted() | Sorts elements in natural order. There is also an overload that takes a Comparator as an argument. |
| peek(Consumer<T>) | Performs a side-effect action on each element but passes them unchanged to the next stage. It is often used for debugging purposes and is not recommended for side-effecting operations in production code. |
distinct() and sorted() are stateful intermediate operations that maintain internal state.
contents.note: You can pass [[Comparator]].reverseOrder() to sorted() to sort in descending order.

contents.title: How Lazy Evaluation Works
contents.body:
**Intermediate operations** are **lazily evaluated**, so no processing occurs until a **terminal operation** is called.
This mechanism allows performance optimization by skipping unnecessary computations.
Using peek(), you can observe the behavior of [[lazy evaluation]]. Without a [[terminal operation]], peek() is also not executed.
Additionally, elements pass through the **pipeline** one at a time (all intermediate operations are executed for each element).
This is called "loop fusion" and is efficient because it does not create intermediate buffers.
When combined with short-circuiting terminal operations (such as findFirst), only the necessary elements are processed and the rest are skipped.
contents.note: The fact that a Stream **pipeline** does not process anything without a **terminal operation** is frequently tested on exams.

quizzes.question: Predict the output of the following code.
quizzes.explanation: filter() keeps elements starting with 'a' ("apple", "avocado"), and map() converts each to uppercase. forEach() outputs each element, so APPLE and AVOCADO are printed.

quizzes.question: Which of the following correctly describes flatMap()?
quizzes.explanation: flatMap() transforms each element into a Stream and merges (flattens) those multiple Streams into a single Stream. While map() performs a one-to-one transformation, flatMap() performs a one-to-many transformation, flattening nested structures.

choices.text: Transforms each element into a Stream and flattens them into a single Stream
Transforms each element in a one-to-one manner

quizzes.question: Predict the output of the following code.
quizzes.explanation: distinct() removes duplicates, resulting in [3, 1, 2, 4] (maintaining encounter order). sorted() sorts in natural order, producing [1, 2, 3, 4]. forEach() prints each element separated by spaces.

lessons.title: Terminal Operations
lessons.summary: Learn about Stream terminal operations: forEach(), count(), collect(), findFirst(), findAny(), anyMatch(), allMatch(), noneMatch(), and reduce(). Terminal operations trigger the actual execution of the Stream pipeline.

contents.title: Overview of Terminal Operations
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Executing actions on each element with forEach()
- Converting results to collections with collect()
- Aggregating with count(), min(), max(), reduce()

**Terminal operations** are the "trigger" of a Stream **pipeline**.

contents.title: forEach(), count(), collect()
contents.body:
| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| forEach(Consumer<T>) | A terminal operation that executes an action on each element; returns void. |
| count() | Returns the number of elements in the Stream as a long. |
| collect(Collector) | The most versatile terminal operation that gathers Stream elements into a collection, etc. Used in combination with various methods of the Collectors class. |
| collect(Supplier, BiConsumer, BiConsumer) | The 3-argument version of collect. Provides fine-grained control over accumulation into a mutable container. |
You can also convert Stream elements to an [[array]] using toArray().
contents.note: The "//" in the code are comments (explanatory notes). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: findFirst(), findAny(), Match Operations
contents.body:
| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| findFirst() | Returns the first element of the Stream as an Optional. Returns Optional.empty() if there are no elements. |
| findAny() | Returns any element of the Stream. In parallel Streams, it may not be the first element. |
| anyMatch(Predicate) | Returns true if at least one element matches the condition. |
| allMatch(Predicate) | Returns true if all elements match the condition. Returns true for an empty Stream. |
| noneMatch(Predicate) | Returns true if no elements match the condition. Returns true for an empty Stream. |
All of these are short-circuiting **terminal operations** that stop processing the remaining elements once the result is determined.
contents.note: allMatch() and noneMatch() return true for an empty Stream. This is called "vacuous truth" and is a point that is commonly tested on exams.

contents.title: reduce()
contents.body:
| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| reduce() | A terminal operation that aggregates Stream elements into a single result. |
| reduce(BinaryOperator<T>) | Combines elements sequentially and returns the result as Optional<T>. |
| reduce(T identity, BinaryOperator<T>) | Allows specifying an initial value; the result is returned as type T. |
With an initial value, even if the Stream is empty, the initial value is returned, so it does not become an [[Optional]].
The 3-argument reduce(identity, [[BiFunction]], [[BinaryOperator]]) is for parallel processing, allowing you to specify a combiner.
reduce() is a general-purpose operation that serves as the foundation for specialized [[terminal operations]] such as sum() and max().
contents.note: When reduce() with an initial value processes an empty Stream, the initial value is returned as-is. Without an initial value, Optional.empty() is returned.

contents.title: Exam Prep: Return Types of Terminal Operations
contents.body:
The return types of **terminal operations** are frequently tested on exams. Memorize the following precisely.
| Method | Return Type |
|---|---|
| forEach() | void |
| count() | long |
| findFirst() / findAny() | Optional<T> |
| anyMatch() / allMatch() / noneMatch() | boolean |
| reduce(BinaryOperator) | Optional<T> |
| reduce(identity, BinaryOperator) | T |
| collect() | Type depends on the Collector |
contents.note: Since forEach() returns void, writing forEach() in the middle of a **pipeline** causes a [[compile]] error. It is a **terminal operation**, not an **intermediate operation**.

quizzes.question: Predict the output of the following code.
quizzes.explanation: filter() keeps elements where n > 4 (5, 8, 9). reduce(0, Integer::sum) adds them sequentially starting from 0: 0+5=5, 5+8=13, 13+9=22. The result is 22.

quizzes.question: What is the return value when calling allMatch(n -> n > 0) on an empty Stream?
quizzes.explanation: allMatch() returns true for an empty Stream. This is a concept known as "vacuous truth" — since there are no elements that violate the condition, the result is true. Similarly, noneMatch() also returns true for an empty Stream. anyMatch() returns false for an empty Stream.

quizzes.question: To get the number of elements in a Stream as a long, use the ___() method.
quizzes.explanation: count() is a terminal operation that returns the number of elements in a Stream as a long. Note that the return type is long, not int.

quizzes.question: Complete the code to extract only elements with 3 or more characters from a string list, convert them to uppercase, and print them using the Stream API.
quizzes.explanation: The Stream intermediate operation filter() narrows down elements matching a condition, and map() transforms them. The terminal operation forEach() processes each element. Method references (::) are used like System.out::println.

lessons.title: Collectors
lessons.summary: Learn the key methods of the Collectors class. Understand collection conversion with toList(), toSet(), toMap(), string concatenation with joining(), and grouping with groupingBy() and partitioningBy().

contents.title: Overview of the Collectors Class
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- How to use Collectors]].[[toList(), toSet(), toMap()
- Grouping with Collectors.groupingBy()
- String concatenation with Collectors.joining()

Comprehensive coverage of how to use Collectors passed as arguments to collect().

contents.title: toList(), toSet(), toMap()
contents.body:
Collectors.toList() converts Stream elements into a List.
Collectors.toSet() converts Stream elements into a Set (duplicates are automatically removed).
Collectors.toMap(keyMapper, valueMapper) converts Stream elements into a Map.
If keys are duplicated with toMap(), an IllegalStateException is thrown.
To handle duplicate keys, specify a mergeFunction (third argument).
You can also generate an unmodifiable collection with Collectors.toUnmodifiableList(), etc. (since Java 10).
contents.note: Since Java 16, the Stream.toList() [[method]] was added, allowing you to write stream().toList() instead of collect(Collectors.toList()).

contents.title: joining()
contents.body:
Collectors.joining() concatenates the string elements of a Stream.
With no arguments, elements are concatenated without a delimiter.
With one argument, elements are concatenated with the specified delimiter.
With three arguments, you can specify a delimiter, a prefix, and a suffix.
It must be used with a Stream of CharSequence; for other types, convert with map() first.
contents.note: The "//" in the code are comments (explanatory notes). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: groupingBy() and partitioningBy()
contents.body:
Collectors.groupingBy(Function) groups elements based on a specified key and returns Map<K, List<T>>.
You can specify a downstream Collector as a second argument to customize the aggregation within each group.
Collectors.partitioningBy([[Predicate]]) divides elements into two groups based on a boolean condition, returning Map<Boolean, List<T>>.
The keys for partitioningBy() are always true and false (even if there are no elements, an empty list is included).
By combining groupingBy() with counting() or summing(), you can perform per-group aggregation.
contents.note: groupingBy() also has a 3-argument version that lets you specify the type of Map to be created (such as [[TreeMap]]).

contents.title: counting() and summarizingInt()
contents.body:
| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| counting() | Counts the number of elements in a group as a Long |
| summingInt(ToIntFunction) | Converts to integers and calculates the sum |
| averagingInt() | Calculates the average |
| summarizingInt(ToIntFunction) | Returns an IntSummaryStatistics containing count/sum/min/average/max |
| maxBy() / minBy() | Gets the maximum/minimum within a group |
These are typically used in combination with groupingBy() as downstream collectors.

quizzes.question: Predict the output of the following code.
quizzes.explanation: Collectors.joining(delimiter, prefix, suffix) concatenates elements with a delimiter and adds a prefix and suffix. Concatenating with "-" gives "A-B-C-D", and adding prefix "{" and suffix "}" results in "{A-B-C-D}".

quizzes.question: What happens by default when duplicate keys occur in Collectors.toMap()?
quizzes.explanation: When duplicate keys occur in Collectors.toMap(), an IllegalStateException is thrown by default. To allow duplicate keys, you need to specify a mergeFunction ((v1, v2) -> ...) as the third argument to define the rule for combining values on duplicate.

choices.text: The later value overwrites the earlier one

quizzes.question: Predict the output of the following code.
quizzes.explanation: groupingBy() groups by the first letter. The 'c' group has 2 elements ("cat" and "car"), and the 'd' group has 2 elements ("dog" and "deer"). Collectors.counting() counts the number of elements in each group, so {c=2, d=2} is output.

quizzes.question: Select all correct terminal operations of the Stream API.
quizzes.explanation: collect(), reduce(), forEach(), count(), and findFirst() are terminal operations. filter() and map() are intermediate operations, not terminal operations. Intermediate operations are lazily evaluated and are only executed when a terminal operation is called.

lessons.title: Optional
lessons.summary: Learn the creation, checking, value retrieval, and transformation methods of the Optional class. Understand creation with of(), ofNullable(), empty(), value retrieval with isPresent() and orElse(), and Optional transformation with map() and flatMap().

contents.title: What Is Optional?
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Writing null-safe code with [[Optional]]<T>
- Distinguishing between isPresent(), ifPresent(), orElse(), and orElseGet()
- Transforming Optionals with map() and flatMap()

Write safe code using Optional instead of null checks.

contents.title: Optional Creation Methods
contents.body:
Optional.of(value) creates an Optional containing a non-null value. Passing null throws a [[NullPointerException]].
Optional.ofNullable(value) creates an Optional from a value that might be null. If the value is null, it behaves the same as empty().
Optional.empty() creates an empty Optional.
Use of() when you are certain the value is not null, and ofNullable() when unsure.
Choosing the appropriate creation [[method]] is the first step to using Optional safely.
contents.note: The "//" in the code are comments (explanatory notes). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: isPresent(), ifPresent(), get()
contents.body:
| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| isPresent() | Returns true if a value is present, false if empty. |
| isEmpty() | A method added in Java 11 that returns the opposite of isPresent(). |
| ifPresent(Consumer) | Executes the Consumer only if a value is present. |
| get() | Retrieves the value, but throws NoSuchElementException if called on an empty Optional. |
It is safe to check with isPresent() before using get(), or to use orElse-family methods.
ifPresentOrElse([[Consumer]], [[Runnable]]) was added in Java 9 as a method that branches processing based on whether a value is present.
contents.note: Since get() throws an [[exception]] on an empty Optional, using orElse() or orElseGet() is recommended.

contents.title: orElse(), orElseGet(), orElseThrow()
contents.body:
| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| orElse(T other) | Returns the value if present, otherwise returns the default value provided as an argument. |
| orElseGet(Supplier<T>) | Returns the value if present, otherwise returns the result of the Supplier. |
| orElseThrow() | Returns the value if present, otherwise throws NoSuchElementException. |
orElseThrow([[Supplier]]<Exception>) allows customizing the type of exception.
The difference between orElse() and orElseGet() is that orElse() always evaluates the default value, while orElseGet() evaluates it only when the Optional is empty.
or(Supplier<Optional>) was added in Java 9 to return a different Optional when empty.
contents.note: When performance matters or the default value is expensive to generate, use orElseGet().

contents.title: Optional's map() and flatMap()
contents.body:
Optional.map([[Function]]) applies the function if a value is present and wraps the result in an Optional.
Calling map() on an empty Optional does nothing and returns Optional.empty().
Optional.flatMap(Function) is similar to map(), but prevents double-wrapping when the function returns an Optional.
The function for map() returns T→R, while the function for flatMap() returns T→Optional<R>.
The stream() method (since Java 9) converts an Optional to a Stream with zero or one element.
contents.note: flatMap() is needed when using functions that return Optional. Using map() would result in Optional<Optional<T>>.

quizzes.question: Predict the output of the following code.
quizzes.explanation: Optional.ofNullable(null) creates Optional.empty(). Calling map() on an empty Optional returns Optional.empty() as-is. orElse(-1) returns the default value -1, so -1 is output.

quizzes.question: What happens when Optional.of(null) is executed?
quizzes.explanation: Optional.of() assumes a non-null value. Passing null throws a NullPointerException. For values that might be null, you need to use Optional.ofNullable().

choices.text: An Optional containing null is created

quizzes.question: The following code has an error. Which line is the cause of the error?
quizzes.explanation: An empty Optional is created with Optional.empty(). Calling get() on an empty Optional throws a NoSuchElementException at runtime. Instead of get(), you should use orElse() or orElseThrow() to safely retrieve the value. Line 2's opt.get() is the cause of the error.

choices.text: Line 2: attempting to get a value from an empty Optional with opt.get()
Line 1: the type parameter of Optional.empty() is invalid
Line 3: toUpperCase() is called on null
Line 1: Optional.empty() is a compile error

lessons.title: Primitive Streams
lessons.summary: Learn about primitive-specialized Streams: IntStream, LongStream, and DoubleStream. Understand range specification with range() and rangeClosed(), aggregation with sum(), average(), max(), and min(), and conversions with mapToInt() and boxed().

contents.title: What Are Primitive Streams?
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- How to create IntStream, LongStream, and DoubleStream
- Generating sequential number Streams with range() and rangeClosed()
- Aggregation operations such as sum(), average(), and max()

Efficient numerical processing that avoids the overhead of [[autoboxing]].

contents.title: IntStream.range() and rangeClosed()
contents.body:
IntStream.range(start, end) generates a Stream of consecutive integers from start to end-1 (exclusive of end).
IntStream.rangeClosed(start, end) generates a Stream of consecutive integers from start to end (inclusive of end).
LongStream.range() and LongStream.rangeClosed() work the same way.
DoubleStream does not have a range() [[method]].
These are useful for expressing processing equivalent to [[for loop]] iterations using the **Stream API**.
contents.note: range(5, 5) is an empty Stream, but rangeClosed(5, 5) is a Stream with one element: 5. This difference is commonly tested on exams.

contents.title: sum(), average(), max(), min()
contents.body:
| Method | Return Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| sum() | int / long / double | Sum of all elements |
| average() | OptionalDouble | Average of all elements. Returns empty() for an empty Stream |
| max() / min() | OptionalInt, etc. | Maximum/minimum value |
| count() | long | Number of elements |
| summaryStatistics() | IntSummaryStatistics, etc. | Gets count, sum, min, average, and max all at once |
contents.note: sum() returns a primitive value directly, but average(), max(), and min() return [[Optional]] types — keep this in mind.

contents.title: mapToInt() and boxed()
contents.body:
To convert from Stream<T> to a primitive Stream, use mapToInt(), mapToLong(), or mapToDouble().
Conversely, to convert from a primitive Stream to Stream<T>, use boxed().
mapToObj() converts a primitive Stream to an arbitrary object Stream.
asLongStream() and asDoubleStream() allow conversion between primitive Streams.
boxed() is needed when you want to use collect(Collectors]].[[toList()), for example.
contents.note: The "//" in the code are comments (explanatory notes). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: Exam Prep: Primitive Stream Caveats
contents.body:
IntStream's collect() differs from Stream<T>'s collect() and cannot directly accept a Collector.
To use Collectors.toList() with IntStream, you need to convert it to Stream<Integer> using boxed() first.
IntStream.of() lets you specify individual values to create a primitive Stream.
Note that the return type of average() is OptionalDouble, not double.
The get() method of OptionalInt is named getAsInt() (different from Optional<T>'s get()).
You cannot call sum() directly on Stream<Integer>. You need to convert it to IntStream using mapToInt(Integer::intValue).
contents.note: OptionalInt uses getAsInt(), OptionalLong uses getAsLong(), and OptionalDouble uses getAsDouble().

quizzes.question: Predict the output of the following code.
quizzes.explanation: IntStream.rangeClosed(1, 5) generates a Stream of integers from 1 to 5 (1, 2, 3, 4, 5). sum() calculates their total: 1+2+3+4+5=15.

quizzes.question: To convert an IntStream to Stream<Integer>, call the ___() method.
quizzes.explanation: The boxed() method is used to convert an IntStream to Stream<Integer>. boxed() returns a Stream where primitive values are boxed into their wrapper class. This enables the use of Stream<T> Collectors such as Collectors.toList().

quizzes.question: What is the result of IntStream.range(3, 3).count()?
quizzes.explanation: IntStream.range(start, end) generates a Stream from start to end-1. Since range(3, 3) has the same start and end, it creates a Stream with 0 elements, and count() returns 0. With rangeClosed(3, 3), a Stream containing only the element 3 would be created, and count() would return 1.
